revision.text:
[[arz:اويشسميزو هاشتشمنجى]][[de:Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]][[en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū]][[es:Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]][[fa:ایواشیمیزو هاچیمانگو]][[fr:Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]][[id:Iwashimizu Hachimangū]][[it:Santuario di Iwashimizu]][[ja:石清水八幡宮]][[ko:이와시미즈 하치만궁]][[la:Iwashimizu Hachimagu]][[my:အိဝရှိမိဇု ဟချိမန်း နတ်ကွန်း]][[pl:Iwashimizu Hachimangū]][[ru:Ивасимидзу-Хатиман-гу]][[simple:Iwashimizu Hachimangū]][[zh:石清水八幡宮]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine
石清水八幡宮 | image = IwashimizuHachimangu.jpg | caption = The Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|52|47|N|135|42|00|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]]
''[[Kokushi genzaisha]]''
''{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}}''
''[[Chokusaisha]]''
''[[Beppyo Shrine]]''
''Shikinaisya''
Former ''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpeitaisha]]'' | deity = [[Hachiman]] | founded_by = | established = 859 | date_destroyed = | location = {{ill|Yawata|en|Yawata|qid=Q260923|lt=Yawata|1=Yawata}} | website = {{URL|http://www.iwashimizu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Hachiman-zukuri|en|Hachiman-zukuri|qid=Q3125455|lt=Hachiman-zukuri|1=Hachiman-zukuri}} | festival = iwashimizu-sai (石清水祭) (September 15th) | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Iwashimizu Hachimangū'''|石清水八幡宮}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Yawata|en|Yawata|qid=Q260923|lt=Yawata|1=Yawata}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. ==History== The shrine's {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} connections with the Kyoto and the Imperial family date from its founding in 859 (''{{ill|Jōgan|en|Jōgan|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 1'')Kanda, Christine Guth. (1985). [https://books.google.com/books?id=xKuEXrKXdOgC&dq=Iwashimizu&pg=PA41 ''Shinzō: Hachiman imagery and Its Development,'' p. 41.] when construction on its earliest structures commenced.Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' (1979). ''Gukanshō,'' p. 288. Shrine tradition explains that {{ill|Emperor Seiwa|en|Emperor Seiwa|qid=Q349243|lt=Emperor Seiwa|1=Emperor Seiwa}} ordered the shrine to be built in obeisance to an oracle in which the god [[Hachiman]] expressed the desire to be near to Kyoto to watch over the city and the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' p. 78. This vision was reported by a Buddhist monk, [[Gyōkyō]], who had a second vision which led to selecting the Otokoyama location where the shrine now stands.Kanda, [https://books.google.com/books?id=xKuEXrKXdOgC&dq=Iwashimizu&pg=PA41 p. 42.] Like other Hachiman shrines, until 1868 Iwashimizu was actually a shrine-temple complex (''{{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|qid=Q1074971|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji}}'') called {{nihongo|Iwashimizu Hachimangū-ji|石清水八幡宮寺}} dedicated to Buddhism as much as to ''kami'' worship.{{EOS|Shinto and Buddhism|826|Satō, Makoto|August 14, 2011}} The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|qid=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Ōharano Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 116-117. The shrine's importance and influence grew in succeeding centuries; and its extensive landholdings led to modest conflicts with [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] during the years in which the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|qid=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}} was establishing itself. The shrine sought to maintain its traditional exemption from contributing to paying the costs of military forces.Maas, Jeffrey P. (1999). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XyH_86xBezoC&dq=Iwashimizu&pg=PA202 ''Yoritomo and the Founding of the First Bakufu: The Origins of Dual Government in Japan,'' p. 202.] In time, the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} faded away; and the shrine endured. ''Iwashimizu Hachimangū'' and ''[[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]]'' were specified for {{nihongo|"the two ancestral mausoleum"|二所宗廟}} in the Middle Ages. * '''1456''' (''Kōshō 2, 3rd month''): {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|en|Ashikaga Yoshimasa|qid=Q447034|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimasa|1=Ashikaga Yoshimasa}} visited [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Shrine]]; and all the officials of the ''[[Daijō-kan]]'' joined him in going there.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran&pg=PP9 p. 348.] From 1871 through 1946, Iwashimizu Hachimangū was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines. Other similarly honored Hachiman shrines were [[Usa Jingū|Usa Shrine]] of {{ill|Usa, Ōita|en|Usa, Ōita|qid=Q114147|lt=Usa|1=Usa, Ōita}} in {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|qid=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}} and [[:en:Hakozaki-gū|Hakozaki-gū]] of [[:en:Fukuoka, Fukuoka|Fukuoka]] in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124-126. ===Imperial progresses to the shrine=== In 979 (''Tengen 2''), [[:en:Emperor Enyū|Emperor Enyū]] visited the shrine; and the shrine continued to be visited by nearly all the emperors until the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|qid=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}, when the sovereigns began to live more secluded lives.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' p. 116. In the ''Shōhei'' era (1346–1370), {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|qid=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} visited Iwashimizu in person.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' p. 218. After the [[:en:Ōnin war|Ōnin war]] (1467–1477), Imperial visits were held in abeyance for 200 years.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Studies, '' p. 244. ==Shinto belief== The shrine is dedicated to the veneration of [[Hachiman]], the [[Shinto]] ''[[kami]]'' or spirit guardian of [[:en:Emperors of Japan|Imperial legitimacy]]. Since the time of its founding in 859, Hachiman has been recognized as [[:en:Emperor Ojin|Emperor Ojin]].Ponsbonby-Fane, ''Studies,'' pp. 78, 196. ==Treasures== A 2005 survey of the treasures at Iwashimizu revealed, among other things, the existence of a ''{{ill|Kris|en|Kris|qid=Q331220|lt=Kris|1=Kris}}'', a jeweled {{ill|Indonesia|en|Indonesia|qid=Q252|lt=Indonesia|1=Indonesia}}n dagger, which was exhibited at {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|en|Kyoto National Museum|qid=Q147286|lt=Kyoto National Museum|1=Kyoto National Museum}} as part of an exhibit entitled "Famous Swords from Kyoto's Temples and Shrines."Kyoto National Museum: [http://www.kyohaku.go.jp/eng/tenji/chinretsu/katana/katana.html 2006 exhibition, treasures] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080423122339/http://www.kyohaku.go.jp/eng/tenji/chinretsu/katana/katana.html |date=2008-04-23 }} ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] ==Notes== {{reflist|33em}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2362-7}}; {{ISBN|9780824823634}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/43487317 OCLC 43487317] * Brown, Delmer M. and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past.''] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/251325323 OCLC 251325323] * Kanda, Christine Guth. (1985). [https://books.google.com/books?id=xKuEXrKXdOgC ''Shinzō: Hachiman imagery and Its Development.''] Cambridge: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|qid=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press|1=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-80650-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=IGkrAAAAIAAJ&q=Vicissitudes+of+Shinto ''Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/36655 OCLC 36655] * Maas, Jeffrey P. (1999). [https://books.google.com/books?id=XyH_86xBezoC ''Yoritomo and the Founding of the First Bakufu: The Origins of Dual Government in Japan.''] Stanford: {{ill|Stanford University Press|en|Stanford University Press|qid=Q1479937|lt=Stanford University Press|1=Stanford University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-3591-9}} * {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|qid=Q332779|lt=Titsingh, Isaac.|1=Isaac Titsingh}} (1834). ''[[:en:Nihon Odai Ichiran|Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691] ==External links== {{commons Category|Iwashimizu Hachimangu|Iwashimizu Hachimangū}} * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www.iwashimizu.or.jp/ Iwashimizu Hachimangū web site] (in Japanese) * [http://www.taleofgenji.org/iwashimizu_hachiman.html Photos of Iwashimizu Hachimangū and references in ancient Japanese literature] {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[image:Iwashimizu Hachiman Shrine - Minami Somon.jpg|thumb|200px|Main gate of the Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] [[en:Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] [[arz:اويشسميزو هاشتشمنجى]] [[de:Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] [[es:Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] [[fr:Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]] [[id:Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] [[it:Santuario di Iwashimizu]] [[ja:石清水八幡宮]] [[ko:이와시미즈 하치만궁]] [[la:Iwashimizu Hachimagu]] [[my:အိဝရှိမိဇု ဟချိမန်း နတ်ကွန်း]] [[ru:Ивасимидзу-Хатиман-гу]] [[simple:Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] [[zh:石清水八幡宮]] {{wikidata link|Q710098}} [[Category:859 establishments]] [[Category:9th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Chokusaisha]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Hachiman-zukuri]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 850s]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
